summary: Dify is a large-scale LLM application platform with a Python/Flask backend, Next.js frontend, and Celery workers. It exposes multiple API layers (console, service API, web, inner API, MCP, webhooks) with varying authentication requirements. Critical attack surfaces include remote code execution via sandboxed executor, file upload/download with SSRF proxy, plugin backwards invocation, webhook triggers accepting unauthenticated input, and a RAG pipeline processing untrusted documents from external sources.

surfaces.description: API-key authenticated workflow execution endpoint that triggers the full workflow graph engine including code execution nodes, HTTP request nodes, and tool invocations
API-key authenticated chat endpoint accepting user messages that flow through LLM, tool invocation, and RAG retrieval pipelines
Unauthenticated webhook trigger endpoint accepting GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE that injects data directly into workflow execution
Plugin endpoint trigger that accepts external requests and routes them to plugin execution with credential passing
Multiple file upload endpoints across service API, web API, and console with varying auth requirements; files processed through type detection and stored via configurable storage backends
Remote file proxy endpoint that fetches content from user-supplied URLs, high SSRF risk despite proxy protections
Plugin file upload endpoint with signature verification; misvalidation could allow unauthorized file uploads to plugin storage
Remote code execution service executing Python3/JavaScript/Jinja2 in external sandbox with network enabled by default; template injection risk via workflow variables
Internal API for plugin-to-host tool invocation; if inner API network boundary is misconfigured, allows unauthenticated tool execution
Internal API for plugin-to-host LLM invocation; relies on network-level isolation rather than token authentication
Internal encryption service for plugin credentials using AES-CBC derived from SECRET_KEY; exposure enables credential decryption
Internal file upload request endpoint for plugins; insufficient boundary enforcement could allow unauthorized file injection
MCP JSON-RPC server endpoint allowing method invocation on registered MCP servers; method dispatch could be abused for unauthorized operations
Executes HTTP requests to external APIs with configurable auth (API key, Bearer token); dynamic URL construction with user-controlled path/query/body parameters
Routes tool invocations to remote plugin daemon with credential passing; plugin isolation depends on tenant_id boundary enforcement
Fetches and processes documents from external URLs via SSRF proxy; supports PDF, Word, Excel, HTML, XML extraction with potential for malicious document exploitation
External website crawling services with stored API keys; crawled content injected into RAG pipeline without sanitization
30+ vector database backend implementations with dynamic query construction; TiDB backend constructs connection strings with credentials, potential SQL injection in parameterized queries
Document creation endpoints accepting file uploads and URL sources for knowledge base indexing; untrusted content flows into embedding and retrieval pipelines
Authentication endpoints handling OAuth callbacks, password resets, and SSO; token validation and session management are critical security boundaries

surfaces.identifier: Tool File Access with HMAC Signing
surfaces.description: File access controlled by HMAC-SHA256 signatures with 30-minute timeout; signature bypass would expose all tool-generated files
HTTP-based MCP client connecting to external tool servers via SSE transport; URL-based routing with SSRF-protected connections
Model provider configuration endpoints accepting API keys and credentials for LLM providers; stored credentials encrypted with SECRET_KEY
Parallel external data fetching via ThreadPoolExecutor to user-configured API endpoints; SSRF and data injection risks from external responses
Workflow publishing endpoint that activates workflow graphs containing code execution, HTTP request, and tool invocation nodes for production use
